published_date: 2026-03-24
video_id: wF-py1heZn0
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wF-py1heZn0
title: The Macro Minute: Did “TACO Trump” just cede control of global energy markets to Iran, part II?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale,
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
March 24th, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. So, as always, we start
with the executive summary from today's
lead off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
did Taco Trump just permanently cede
control of global energy markets to
Iran? Part two.
The answer is, this outcome appears
increasingly likely without risking
another forever war in the Middle East.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Starting with
the growth cycle. The weak negative
impulse in the final Q4 nonfarm
productivity data and strong positive
impulse in the final Q4 unit labor cost
data did not support our productivity
boom theme. The federal government
shutdown, which represented a greater
than 100 basis point drag on headline
GDP growth, was a contributing factor.
The strong positive impulses in headline
and residential construction spending in
January supported our paradigm C theme.
For those who may be unfamiliar,
paradigm C is the the grow phase of the
cut grow print sequence required to
address the geopolitically driven supply
demand imbalance in the Treasury bond
market, which has been our core research
theme and thesis since the summer of
2023. Elsewhere, leading indicator
signal a nascent trend of slowing global
growth via the preliminary March
composite PMI data. In the Eurozone, the
March composite PMI, March consumer
confidence, February auto and February
auto sales data generally supported
outlook for moderating growth. In Japan,
the March composite PMI data supported
our outlook for moderating growth and it
so as well in the UK.
Regarding the inflation cycle, as we
predicted, Iran has begun charging
vessels for safe passage through the
Strait of Hormuz. The levies, reaching
up to 2 million thus far, are being
solicited on a discretionary basis,
effectively institutionalizing an
informal tolling regime. Select vessels
have complied with these payments
despite ambiguity surrounding execution
mechanics, including currency
denomination. While currently
implemented on a transactional basis,
Iranian authorities have signaled
potential formalization of these transit
fees within a broader post-war
framework. The US administration will
have no choice but to risk another
forever war in the Middle East to
counter this mafia-style takeover of the
world's most important shipping lane.
Elsewhere, in Japan, the February CPI
data did not support our outlook for
firming inflation. With regards to the
monetary policy cycle, Japan's February
CPI data materially threatened Japan's
years-long reflation initiative and
support a slower pace of hawkish
monetary policy normalization. With
regards to fiscal policy, Japan's
February CPI data materially threatened
Japan's years-long reflation initiative
and supports a faster pace of fiscal
easing
by the Takiji government.
Transition to liquidity. Equities across
alternative asset managers declined
after Ares and Apollo imposed new
withdrawal restrictions on select funds.
Apollo disclosed late Monday that it is
restricting redemptions in its $25
billion retail focused private credit
vehicle, Apollo Debt Solutions, capping
withdrawals at 5% of outstanding shares
after investors requested 11.2%.
Ares similarly limited redemptions in
its $10.7 billion Ares Strategic Income
Fund, enforcing a 5% withdrawal cap
after clients sought to redeem 11.6%.
These pockets of illiquidity risk
continue to spill over into broader
financial markets as panicked investors
will be forced to continue selling what
they can, not what they desire to get
out of.
This is not just a retail investor
phenomenon. Central banks are struggling
with illiquidity, too. For example,
Turkey's central bank is assembling an
expanded intervention toolkit to
stabilize the lira against
geopolitically driven volatility,
including potential mobilization of a
substantial of its substantial gold
reserves.
Officials have evaluated executing
before and currency swaps in London and
made gold holdings at the Bank of
England to facilitate FX intervention
operations. And then finally,
positioning. High volume trading
activity in oil and equity futures,
representing billions and billions of
dollars, occurred 15 minutes before a
social media post from President Trump
triggered a sharp decline in crude oil
prices and a rally in stocks. Exchange
data show contracts equivalent to at
least 6 million dollar barrels of Brent
and WTI
were sold within 2 minutes at 6:49 a.m.
in New York time Monday versus a prior
prior 5-day average of roughly 700,000
barrels. 6 million barrels versus the
5-day average of 700,000 barrels.
With President Trump's Truth Social post
following at approximately 7:05 a.m. US
equity futures also saw a surge, 6,000
S&P 500 contracts traded, exceeding 2
billion in notional value, marking a
significant deviation from otherwise
views pre-market activity. This
anomalous volume pattern extended across
global futures markets, including
Germany's DAX index futures, Euro Stoxx
50 index futures, Nasdaq index futures,
Russell 2000 index futures, as well as
natural gas contracts.
We included this information to remind
investors of three things. One, someone
always knows something.
Two, the morally impaired will always
use material nonpublic information,
MNPI,
you know, to make money. Obviously, you
got your folks
Pelosi family office and the Trump meme
coins saga from last year, you know,
just to name a couple of
a couple of
instances
on both sides of the aisle. And number
three,
because of this, the fact that someone
always knows something and the morally
impaired will always use MNPI to make
money, to game the system. Because of
this, investors need effective risk
management systems to accelerate the
process of making quality risk
management decisions, particularly in
and around what we call row row phase
transitions in asset markets. Row row
means going from a risk on regime to a
risk off regime or a risk off regime to
a risk on regime. And so, when you're
having these row row phase transitions,
you need something to tell you to take
up risk heading into a market
risk on market regime faster than you
otherwise would using fundamental
research or you need a system
to tell you to take down risk heading
into a risk off market regime faster
than you otherwise would. Investors who
wait on macro or micro data to have
their legacy views and positions
disconfirmed will always and
increasingly arrive late to developing
market trends. And emphasis on always
and increasingly because we know the
game is always getting faster with the
use of technology, particularly AI. So,
as always, continue reviewing to learn
whether our institutional grade risk
management models, KISS and Dr. Mo, are
evolving their signals in response to
these anticipated fundamental dynamics.
